title: simple start
content_html:
simple start

Start again from a very simple implementation that just creates a simple composite from 3 source UHD images, each layer at 50% opacity. The source folder and destination folders are hard coded.

we’ll strip things right back to basics. Here’s a minimal Processing sketch that: • Loads exactly 3 UHD images (3840 × 2160). • Blends them in order, each new layer at 50% opacity on top of the previous one. • Uses hard-coded source and output paths. • Saves one composite PNG to the output folder.

⸻

*// SimpleComposite3_UHD.pde*




*// Minimal example: 3 UHD images, blended at 50% opacity each.*




*// Source and output folders are hard-coded.*



String sourceDir = "/Users/yourname/Pictures/source_uHD/";   

*// <-- change to your folder*


String outputDir = "/Users/yourname/Pictures/output_uHD/";   

*// <-- change to your folder*



PImage img1, img2, img3;

void setup() {
  size(3840, 2160);  

*// UHD canvas*


  surface.setVisible(false);  

*// no display window*



  

*// Load 3 images*


  img1 = loadImage(sourceDir + "image1.png");
  img2 = loadImage(sourceDir + "image2.png");
  img3 = loadImage(sourceDir + "image3.png");

  

*// Ensure UHD and matching dimensions*


  img1.resize(width, height);
  img2.resize(width, height);
  img3.resize(width, height);

  

*// Base image*


  PImage composite = img1.copy();

  

*// Blend in second at 50%*


  composite = blendTwo(composite, img2, 0.5);

  

*// Blend in third at 50%*


  composite = blendTwo(composite, img3, 0.5);

  

*// Save result*


  String outPath = outputDir + "composite.png";
  composite.save(outPath);
  println("Saved composite to: " + outPath);

  exit();
}



*// Simple alpha blend (normal mode, opacity 0..1)*


PImage blendTwo(PImage base, PImage top, float opacity) {
  PImage out = createImage(base.width, base.height, ARGB);
  base.loadPixels();
  top.loadPixels();
  out.loadPixels();
  for (int i = 0; i < base.pixels.length; i++) {
    color cb = base.pixels[i];
    color ct = top.pixels[i];
    float r = red(cb)   * (1 - opacity) + red(ct)   * opacity;
    float g = green(cb) * (1 - opacity) + green(ct) * opacity;
    float b = blue(cb)  * (1 - opacity) + blue(ct)  * opacity;
    out.pixels[i] = color(r, g, b);
  }
  out.updatePixels();
  return out;
}

⸻

👉 This is the clean “ground zero” implementation. Would you like me to also prepare a short HTML note explaining this stripped-down workflow (3 images, fixed paths, 50% opacity)?

Here you go: • Download: Minimal 3-UHD Composite explainer (HTML)

This file explains the stripped-down workflow: 3 source UHD images, 50% opacity blending, hard-coded paths, and saving a single composite.

Want me to also give you the .pde file alongside this HTML so you can drop it straight into Processing?

Here’s the ready-to-use Processing sketch file: • Download: SimpleComposite3_UHD.pde

This matches the HTML description: 3 hard-coded UHD images, each blended at 50% opacity, saved as a single composite.

Do you want me to also prepare a version that works with JPEGs (not just PNGs) by default?

Yes. The png files may have transparency. The jpegs will not. Does this affect the code? Recreate the html explanation

Great point. Here’s the situation: • PNG inputs → may include transparency (alpha channel). Processing loads them with ARGB, so transparent pixels will contribute less (e.g., fully transparent = ignored). • JPEG inputs → no alpha channel, always fully opaque. Processing loads them as RGB but internally fills alpha = 255, so they’re treated as solid.

👉 In your minimal code, you’re blending with a fixed opacity (0.5) and **ignoring alpha**. That means both PNGs and JPEGs are treated the same: fully opaque, except transparent PNG regions still let the lower layer show through. No changes are needed for JPEG support.

⸻

Here’s the updated HTML explanation, now mentioning PNG/JPEG handling: • Download: Minimal 3-UHD Composite explainer (with PNG/JPEG note)
